speaker: interviewer
text: [? Now, ?] you came to Los Angeles. And what positions are you holding in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: I held the position of regional director for the Union of American Hebrew Congregations, being in charge of coordinating the activities of all the Reform congregations affiliated with the UAHC in the six Western states.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what other positions are you holding now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, since I held that position for only three years, from 1946 to 1949, in 1949, I became one of the rabbis at the Wilshire Boulevard Temple, which is the oldest synagogue in Los Angeles and one of the largest and most influential Reform synagogues in the United States. The senior rabbi at that time was Edgar F. Magnin, who had been ordained at the Hebrew Union College in 1915 and who lived to the age of 94, having held that position for-- for 69 years, probably the longest any rabbi has ever held, at least in a Reform synagogue, and a most unusual person. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] To work with him as I did for 36 years was a real privilege, as well as a-- a real task. But the first three years in Los Angeles were very exciting years in that I was able to make contact not only with the rabbis living in Los Angeles, again notably Rabbi Magnin, but with all my colleagues in-- in the Western states, many of them living relatively isolated lives, though none as isolated as I was Dothan, Alabama. And-- but I was able to establish contacts and create links between those congregations, arrange for meetings of the leadership of those congregations. My major task, however, was right here in Southern California, where a very large number of people had moved immediately after the end of the war. The cities in southern California had grown tremendously, and among the stream of people that came here was a very large number of Jews. Therefore, most of the Jews in Los Angeles lived in areas which were very far from the few existing congregations. In all of Southern California, there were six Reform congregations at the time when I came, of which only two were-- three, rather, were in the Los Angeles area itself-- Los Angeles, Beverly-- Hollywood, and Beverly Hills. The-- and my major job was to find groups of Jews in newly settled areas and to help them to form congregations. As I said, there were six congregations affiliated with the Reform movement in Southern California between Santa Barbara and San Diego at the time when I took the job. Three years later, when I left that position for Wilshire Boulevard Temple, there were 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that gives you a little idea of what I had to do, because as long as a congregation was in the process of organization, my office was responsible for giving rabbinic service to that congregation. And at times, there were as many as five or six congregations that I had to oversee, where I had to speak at different places every Sabbath.

speaker: interviewee
text: And where I couldn't go, I had to find somebody for-- who was able to occupy the pulpit for those evenings or the-- and most of them started with just Friday night services, and then later they added Sabbath morning services.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in the military at all? In the military? Were You in the military during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was not. I volunteered, and I was eventually called and passed the necessary examination and was measured for Unit 4, a lieutenant's uniform. But before they finished the uniform, the war ended, and I did not have to leave my family. But I served as civilian chaplain in four different establishments, a very large infantry camp and three Air Force installations. So I-- I did some volunteer work for the-- the war effort, both by serving on those bases and also by welcoming large numbers of soldiers on leave in my congregation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a large family? How-- how-- how many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children started coming a year or so after I went to-- to Dothan. My oldest son was born in August of '42. Then my daughter, who unfortunately is no longer alive, was born in March of '45. And my younger son, Dan, who is visiting with us today, was born in 1950. That's my immediate family. And I have four grandchildren, two children of my oldest son who are now in their late 20s, and two children of my younger son who are 15 and 10 respectively.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest is-- [LAUGHS] this is the second time this has happened somehow-- somehow-- Ben. The-- the-- the second of-- of David's, son is Eric. The older of Dan's children is Aaron, and the youngest, who is my only girl grandchild, is Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was-- became ill with cancer. And after a year of constantly more severe disease and several surgeries, she died in 1987.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she married?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was married during the time of her illness to a wonderful person who was a source of tremendous strength to us during that long illness and who, together with his second wife, is still part of our extended family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name, married name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her married name was Judith Lee, Judith Wolf Lee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rabbi Wolf, is there anything you would like to say that-- that I-- I didn't think about?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the one- the one overwhelming fact to me is that I am a very, very fortunate person that God has blessed me in many ways, starting from birth, having surrounded me with a-- by what they like to call a nurturing family, where I was the-- the only and always spoiled, but I think not excessively spoiled, child, for not only my loving parents, but my grandparents and my resident aunt, to say nothing of the visiting relatives, as well as the-- the visiting-- the visiting salesmen to my parents' store, who also helped to spoil me. I had an excellent education with good teachers. The change in the German government didn't come until after I had experienced all that the enlightened Weimar Republic could offer in its educational system. And that at that point, at an early moment, I had the good fortune of coming to America and then have all the blessings that a democratic America could offer to a new citizen. And then, as an immigrant, having the kind of career that I had, again, was a-- a blessing that I must be grateful for every moment. And the greatest blessing of all, to have my own family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wonderful children, wonderful grandchildren, having in-laws that are very much a harmonious part of the family. My-- Miriam's mother was one of the most unusual, positive personalities that I've ever met and to whom I could become a real son and who could become a real mother to me, especially at the time when my parents were still overseas, but also for the rest of her life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I think I left my parents. And when they picked up their visa in Marseille in 1941, they then made their way across the Mediterranean to Morocco, were able to get on what may have been the last ship chartered by the JVC from Dakar. And after a long trip on-- on the most miserable ship that I ever-- ever visited, because I saw the ship when it-- when it landed in New York, they landed in New York in early '42. I met them in New York. My-- so did my uncle, who by that time was a resident in New York. And my parents then came for a short time to Dothan and later moved to Cincinnati where my aunt was living. And my grandmother also came from New York to Cincinnati. And that's where they spent the rest of their lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I beg your pardon. You know what?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I misspoke. No, they-- they stayed in-- in Cincinnati until after I came to Wilshire Boulevard Temple, and I was fairly certain that I would be settled for-- for good. And then my parents and my grandmother moved to-- to Los Angeles, where they were later joined by my aunt and her husband. And they spent the rest of their lives in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like you to tell us what message you would like to leave for your grandchildren and for future generations in general.

speaker: interviewee
text: Have faith, faith in the future, regardless of whether you believe in God or a deity, or whether you have some other kind of-- of belief. But realize that you are here for a purpose and do your very best to fulfill that purpose to the extent of your possibilities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Rabbi. Rabbi Wolf, would you introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like you to meet my beloved wife, Miriam. We've been married for 56 years. And our youngest offspring, now a grown man and the-- a vice president of the Disney Corporation, Dan Wolf, and a much growing young lady, our one granddaughter, Sarah Wolf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Wolf, when you got married, did you know what the situation was with his family back in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I always say that he married me for my money, because I had $300. And if we were married, we could have a joint account, and he could show that he was a responsible citizen and could sign for his parents to come to this country. Yes, I was-- that's why I got married, because it was-- you helped any way you could, and it wasn't easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come from a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- strictly Reform Judaism. I was born in Ohio. My grandfather, both grandfathers, were members of Orthodox synagogues. But my one grandfather also was one of the people that started the Reform temple in Dayton. And I taught Sunday school. Yes, my father died when I was 11. I went to services twice a week. And yes, I-- I think it was a very religious family. I don't know how an Orthodox person would feel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, did you know that the-- that the-- there was a Holocaust? Did-- did you hear about what was going on at your grandfather-- at your father's country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was-- I don't remember ever not being aware of the Holocaust and certainly of the role it played in my coming to be, because my father leaving and all that. So it was very ingrained in me. It's not like some people, I understand, who was-- were shielded from it in their youth, and then it was almost a surprise to learn about it. So it was always something I was aware of. But of course, in this case, it was-- the role it played in my family history was as people who escaped it, and not people who suffered through it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your grandparents tell you stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, my grandfather never learned English very well. My grandmother did, and I remember talking to her once. Because the camp that they were at in France before they escaped or got out through Morocco, and I remember it was interesting because she-- she apparently was the leader of her area, you know, the way it was assigned. And she was actually almost proud of that fact that if she had to be there, she made the best of it. And she even talked about these beautiful sunrises they saw there where they were in France. But of course, it was all changed with the bitterness that they had to leave what they had built back in Germany. But they went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friends whose parents were survivors of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not close friends. I certainly encountered peers who had that background. But of my closest friends, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about you? What do you want to say to your grandparents now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, tell them about camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to overnight camp the summer that my grandpa started, and it was very fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: But is there anything you want to say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: How you feel about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're great grandparents, and I love them a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I wanted to hear.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Rabbi Wolf, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] is there anything that you would still like to tell us at this time about your work or any message that you would like to leave for humanity?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be down to earth. The-- I am quite proud of having had a-- a-- an experience of more than 50 years in the rabbinate, during which I was able to make some rather unusual contributions to the people whom I served. This includes my putting the finishing touches to the one synagogue in-- in Dothan, Alabama, which still is the-- the only synagogue within 120 miles of that-- of that place and with which I still have some contact. The-- secondly, in my second position as a-- as the director of the Western region for the Union of American Hebrew Congregations, I was able to stimulate the birth and early growth of some six or eight-- no, more than that, about-- about 12 congregations, most of which grew into significant congregations right here in Los Angeles, including Temple Beth Hillel, Temple Isaiah, which are today considered among the well-established older congregations in-- in Los Angeles. And finally, during my 36 years at Wilshire Boulevard Temple, starting at the very beginning of those years, I was able to-- to begin the organization of two very successful institutions, namely Camp Hess Kramer and Gindling Hilltop Camp, two twin camps which are an essential-- an integral part of Wilshire Boulevard Temple now, which each summer are home to more than 1,000 children who are getting a combination of a healthy summer vacation and a very thorough Jewish education. In addition, those camps have been used by both youth and adult groups during the year, groups of-- representing every religious and racial background in the community, but all of them groups that are-- add to the culture and the welfare of the community. If I had done nothing else within those 36 years but to get those two camps going, I think my rabbinic life would have been worthwhile. But of course, in addition, I did all the things that rabbis are supposed to do. And every time I see somebody or somebody seeks me out in a crowd and tells me of what I've done for him or her as the rabbi, I glow with pride. Because you do get the unusual opportunity of touching people in this position as a rabbi. One other overreaching subject is my work in interfaith relations, which, in a sense, goes right back to my ideas-- to ideas that I picked up in my youth in Germany. I am not quite sure whether it was after, just after, or just before Hitler took over in Germany that I arranged for my high school class to visit our synagogue. And my father came along with the group, so that in addition to whatever I was able to tell them, that he could give them some idea of what Judaism was and stood for. Of course, in the Germany of 1900-- and this must have been about 1932, it was very important to increase the understanding and the goodwill. And in this country, I was involved in interreligious work from the moment I got here, even as a student. And as a rabbi, I was able to be one of the founders and the initial president of the Interreligious Council of Southern California, which includes all the major religions of Southern California.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Thank all of you. OK? My grandfather Benjamin Levy, my grandmother Sophy David Levy. The picture was taken in Germany in the 1930s. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Regina Levy Wolf and Herman Wolf. The picture was taken in Germany in the early '30s. And this is I, Alfred Wolf, taken in Germany on probably my fifth birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: An interfaith service in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the one in the center. It was in Dothan, Alabama. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Alfred Wolf. My parents Herman Wolf and Regina Levy Wolf in-- in Los Angeles, California in 1954. First row, myself, Alfred Wolf, my wife Miriam Wolf, granddaughter Sarah, grandson [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Aaron. The second row, grandson Ben, grandson Eric, daughter-in-law Ruth, daughter-in-law [? Ellie, ?] son Dan, and finally, son David in-- on-- in-- on June 16, 1990.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in-- it's in California, but I don't where.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what occasion? Case

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on our golden wedding anniversary. This is the house in which I was born in Eberbach, Germany photographed after its destruction by an American bombing attack.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
